weekly.angle: Measuring the Mask: How our assessment tools dictate what we assume personality is, setting the epistemological baseline for the entire course.
must_cover: The epistemological stakes of measurement: Choosing an assessment method inherently defines what personality is assumed to be.
The Ergodic Fallacy (Phan et al.): Why population-level trait averages cannot be mathematically or logically applied to individual psychological functioning.
The TRI Model's split between Identity and Reputation: Introducing an early crack in the idea of a unified 'inner core' personality.
avoid: Getting bogged down in the technical mechanics of Machine Learning algorithms or psychometric formulas.
Listing all 25 of Phan et al.'s polytomies for research design.
Treating self-reports as 'true' and observer reports as 'biased' (or vice versa); frame them as distinct epistemological choices.
grounding: Use Phan et al. to ground the critique of population averages (Ergodic Fallacy).
Use Mayer & Bryan to establish the foundational taxonomy of Personal-source vs. External-source data.
Use Koutsoumpis to show how Machine Learning reverses the traditional paradigm by using behavior to predict traits.

per_reading.angle: The taxonomy of personality data and the myth of perfect convergence.
must_cover: The fundamental division between Personal-source data (inside the person) and External-source data (surroundings).
How different measurement methods elicit distinct 'response processes', explaining why they often don't correlate.
The distinction between self-estimated intelligence and actual ability-measured intelligence.
avoid: Assuming that a lack of convergence between two measures means one is invalid.

per_reading.angle: The mathematical limits of population averages in psychology.
must_cover: The Ergodic Fallacy: The danger of applying group-level statistics to individual persons.
The 2x3 matrix replacing the outdated nomothetic-idiographic distinction.
The crucial difference in 'Focal Entity' (Person vs. Population).
avoid: Attempting to explain or memorize the 25 specific polytomous criteria; focus strictly on the conceptual matrix.

per_reading.angle: Reversing the paradigm with Machine Learning Personality Assessment (ML-PA).
must_cover: How ML-PA uses behavior as the independent variable to predict traits as the dependent variable.
The SOKA model: Why ML is more accurate at predicting observer-reported traits than self-reported traits.
The trade-off between applying bias mitigation and maximizing predictive performance.
avoid: Technical explanations of supervised machine learning algorithms.
Assuming ML-PA captures the full variance of questionnaire-based traits.

per_slide.angle: The methodological primer for personality psychology.
must_cover: The TRI Model (Trait-Reputation-Identity) as a framework for multi-rater assessment.
The distinction between random errors and systematic biases in assessment.
The reality of effect sizes in personality psychology (correlations typically ranging from .15 to .40).
avoid: Over-explaining the interactive classroom activities (e.g., 'generate original uses') which lack context in audio format.

short.angle: The Ergodic Fallacy and the TRI Model: Why population averages and self-reports don't tell the whole story of who you are.
must_cover: The Ergodic Fallacy (Phan et al.) explained simply.
The tension between Identity (who I think I am) and Reputation (who others say I am) via the TRI model.
avoid: Technical psychometrics, machine learning mechanics, and detailed research design matrices.

selected_concepts.why: It is a crucial conceptual anchor for the entire course, explaining the logical limits of applying population-level trait averages to individual psychological functioning.
Defines the primary epistemological divide in how personality data is collected, setting up the contrast between subjective experience and objective observation.
Introduces an early structural crack in the idea of a unified 'inner core', preparing students for the decentering of the subject in later psychoanalytic and poststructural modules.
Represents a modern methodological shift that reverses the traditional paradigm by using behavioral exhaust to predict traits.

selected_tensions.why: Highlights that self-reports and observer reports capture fundamentally different facets of a person, proving that personality is not a single, easily captured object.
The core stakes of the Ergodic Fallacy; emphasizes that knowledge about a population average does not automatically translate to knowledge about a specific individual.

grounding_notes: Always frame measurement choices as epistemological choices (what we assume personality is), not just technical or statistical decisions.
Use the Ergodic Fallacy as a deliberate bridge to foreshadow the need for idiographic, phenomenological, and critical theories later in the course.
When discussing ML-PA, explicitly reference the SOKA model to explain why algorithms are better at predicting how others see us (reputation) rather than how we see ourselves (identity).

source_selection.why: Anchor source. Provides the comprehensive classification framework for personality measurement and explains method variance through distinct response processes.
Major source. Introduces the Ergodic Fallacy, which is a vital critical tool for students to use when evaluating trait psychology throughout the course.
Major source. Modernizes the assessment discussion by introducing computational methods and the epistemological implications of using behavioral exhaust to predict traits.
Supporting source. Contextualizes the readings with the TRI model and establishes the baseline expectations for effect sizes in personality research.
